perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it would be difficult to measure the damage to such party from the breach of the other party's obligations hereunder, that injury to such party from any such breach would be impossible to calculate, and that monetary damages would therefor be an inadequate remedy; accordingly, each party agrees that the other party shall be entitled, in addition to all other remedies it might have, to injunctions or other appropriate orders to restrain any such breach without showing or proving any actual damage.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges that measuring damages resulting from a breach of the other party's obligations may be challenging. The parties agree that monetary damages may be considered an option, but the other party might seek injunctions or other orders to restrain any such breach, with the need to demonstrate actual damage at the court's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces ambiguity regarding the availability of injunctive relief. By stating that monetary damages 'may be considered an option,' it opens the door to arguments that monetary damages are sufficient, potentially limiting the ability to obtain an injunction. The phrase 'with the need to demonstrate actual damage at the court's discretion' makes the threshold for obtaining an injunction unclear, as the original clause explicitly waived the requirement to show or prove any actual damage.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3422 outlines specific situations where an injunction can be granted. By adding ambiguity regarding the necessity to prove actual damages before obtaining an injunction, the modified text could be interpreted as contradicting the principles under which injunctive relief is generally available in California, especially in cases involving confidential information and trade secrets, where the immediate and irreparable harm can be difficult to quantify monetarily.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3422. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Preventive Relief [3420 - 3424] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 3422. Except where otherwise provided by this Title, a final injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation existing in favor of the applicant: 1. Where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief; 2. Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief; 3. Where the restraint is necessary to prevent a multiplicity of judicial proceedings; or, 4. Where the obligation arises from a trust. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification:
The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating that monetary damages 'may be considered an option,' suggesting injunctive relief is not the primary or guaranteed remedy. It also introduces uncertainty regarding the need to prove actual damages for an injunction, stating it's at 'the court's discretion.' This directly contradicts the original text, which explicitly stated that parties are entitled to injunctions 'without showing or proving any actual damage.'

Snippet 1 (CIV 3422) states that a final injunction may be granted where 'pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief' or 'where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation.' The modified text undermines the original contract's intent to bypass the need to prove inadequate monetary compensation, thus creating a legal contradiction by potentially requiring a showing of actual damage when the original agreement waived this requirement.

perturbation.original_text: Each party will have employees to whom Confidential Information of the other party is disclosed sign a Non-Disclosure Agreement in content substantially similar to this agreement if such persons have not already signed such agreements obligating them to hold the Confidential Information in confidence.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will encourage employees to whom Confidential Information of the other party is disclosed to consider signing a Non-Disclosure Agreement. The content of such agreements may or may not substantially resemble this agreement, depending on the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the obligation to ensure employee confidentiality. Replacing 'will have ... sign' with 'will encourage ... to consider signing' makes the signing of an NDA optional. Adding that the content 'may or may not substantially resemble this agreement' introduces uncertainty about the enforceability and scope of the employee NDA, undermining the protection of confidential information. The strength of the original agreement's enforcement is now weakened.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By making employee NDAs optional and allowing for potentially dissimilar agreements, the company is not taking reasonable measures to protect the confidentiality of trade secrets as required by California law. It is no longer a requirement and an 'encouragement' is not strong enough of a mandate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated employees sign an NDA with content substantially similar to the agreement, protecting confidential information. The changed text weakens this obligation, only encouraging employees to *consider* signing an NDA, and allows the content of the NDA to be dissimilar, undermining the confidentiality protection. The Law Explanation states that CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The changed text creates a contradiction because 'encouraging' employees to consider signing an NDA, which may or may not be similar to the original agreement, is not a 'reasonable effort' under CUTSA to protect confidential information as required by California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code 3426, which introduces the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. While the snippet itself does not define 'reasonable efforts,' the Law Explanation uses this as the basis for why the change weakens the protection of confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to immediately notify the other party in writing of any misuse or misappropriation of such Confidential Information of the other party which may come to its attention.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to, when feasible, notify the other party of possible misuse or misappropriation of Confidential Information of the other party which may come to its attention.
perturbation.explanation: The insertion of 'when feasible' makes the notification requirement discretionary rather than mandatory. Replacing 'immediately' with no set timeframe introduces ambiguity and allows for delays in notification. Changing 'any' to 'possible' reduces the scope of the reporting obligation, potentially resulting in unreported incidents. The original clause obligated both parties to act fast, but now they might drag their feet if a problem comes to their attention.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.1(b) pertains to the statute of limitations for misappropriation of trade secrets. Delaying notification of misuse or misappropriation can significantly hinder the ability of the disclosing party to take timely legal action to protect their trade secrets within the statute of limitations. By weakening the notification obligation, the amended clause could undermine the prompt action necessary to comply with the legal requirements for protecting trade secrets under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity and discretion where the original contract was clear and mandatory. The original text required immediate written notification of 'any' misuse, whereas the modified text requires notification 'when feasible' of 'possible' misuse. This weakening undermines the ability to protect trade secrets. California Civil Code § 3426.1 defines 'misappropriation' and implies a need for timely action to prevent ongoing harm, but the modified text allows for delays and incomplete reporting. Snippet 1 provides the legal definition of misappropriation, and the perturbation explanation clearly indicates how that definition could now be undermined by the change.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it will take all reasonable steps to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information of the other party on order to prevent of unauthorized falling into the public domain or the possession of unauthorized persons.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will attempt to take steps to protect the confidentiality of the other party's Confidential Information, as circumstances permit.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the obligation significantly. Replacing 'all reasonable steps' with 'attempt to take steps' makes the obligation less stringent. Adding 'as circumstances permit' introduces a large degree of discretion, allowing a party to avoid taking protective measures based on their own interpretation of the circumstances. The original agreement ensured action will be taken, but now action is at the whim of 'circumstances'.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code section 3426.1(d) states that a continuing misappropriation constitutes a single claim of action. This means that if reasonable measures are not taken to protect the secrecy of confidential information and prevent further disclosure once a misappropriation has begun, a party might be deemed to have failed to mitigate damages. By modifying the confidentiality clause, this may be seen as a violation.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation to protect confidential information, potentially leading to a violation of trade secret laws. The original text mandates 'all reasonable steps,' while the changed text uses 'attempt to take steps, as circumstances permit,' significantly reducing the commitment. California Civil Code section 3426.1 defines 'misappropriation' and implies a duty to maintain secrecy, which the altered text undermines. Specifically, subsection (b)(2)(B)(ii) refers to circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy. By adding 'as circumstances permit', the changed text creates ambiguity about whether the duty to maintain secrecy will always be upheld, directly contradicting the intent to protect trade secrets as described in the statute.
